title: Snake bite or sudden facial swelling
text: Suspected snake bite, especially on the head, muzzle, neck, or leg, is an emergency concern. Watch for fast swelling, pain, weakness, collapse, breathing difficulty, bleeding, or nervous signs. Keep the animal calm and avoid running, cutting, sucking, tying tight bands, burning, or applying chemicals. Call trained veterinary help quickly.

title: Snake bite myths to block
text: For suspected snake bite, do not advise cutting the bite, sucking venom, tight tourniquets, burning, black-stone treatment, alcohol, kerosene, or herbal antidote claims. These can delay care or worsen injury. Keep the animal quiet, reduce movement, and escalate to trained help.

title: Pesticide or chemical poisoning suspicion
text: Suspected pesticide, insecticide, herbicide, or chemical exposure is urgent, especially with drooling, tremors, diarrhea, breathing trouble, collapse, convulsions, or several animals affected. Remove animals from the suspected source if it can be done safely. Do not give home antidotes, oil, alcohol, or human medicines. Contact trained help and preserve the label/container information if safe.

title: Suspicious plant or unknown feed poisoning
text: If poisoning is suspected after unknown plants, spoiled feed, sprayed fodder, or unusual feed, stop access to the suspected source and observe how many animals are affected. Red flags include trembling, repeated salivation, severe diarrhea, bloating, collapse, convulsions, or fast breathing. Do not diagnose the poison or give antidotes yourself; seek trained help.

title: Electrocution or lightning safety
text: If an animal may have been electrocuted or struck near live wire/lightning, first protect people. Do not touch the animal or wire until power danger is removed. Once safe, keep the area clear and call trained help. Collapse, burns, abnormal breathing, or not getting up after shock is urgent.

title: Severe bleeding or deep wound
text: Heavy bleeding, a deep open wound, horn injury, exposed tissue, or a wound near the eye, abdomen, udder, or joint needs urgent trained help. Keep handling gentle and clean. Do not pack the wound with soil, ash, dung, powder, chili, kerosene, or unknown chemicals. Avoid rough pulling or probing.

title: Fracture or cannot bear weight
text: If a leg looks deformed, the animal cannot bear weight, bone may be broken, or swelling/pain is severe after injury, treat it as urgent. Keep the animal calm and prevent crowding or forced walking. Do not try to set the bone, twist the limb, or tie tight splints without trained guidance.

title: Eye injury or sudden eye problem
text: Eye injury, sudden cloudiness, swelling, bleeding, severe watering, visible wound, or animal keeping the eye closed needs careful handling and trained help. Do not put chili, ash, human eye drops, oil, or unknown medicine in the eye. Keep the animal away from dust and rough rubbing while arranging help.

title: Afterbirth or placenta not passed
text: After calving, placenta/afterbirth hanging or not passing for many hours, especially with fever, bad smell, not eating, weakness, or restlessness, needs trained help. Do not pull it forcefully, cut it, insert hands, or apply home remedies. Keep the animal clean and calm and call veterinary support.

title: Bad-smelling discharge after calving
text: After calving, bad-smelling discharge, fever, not eating, severe weakness, repeated restlessness, or inability to stand are red flags. Do not give antibiotics, uterus-cleaning medicine, or internal treatment instructions yourself. Keep the animal clean, provide calm space, and arrange trained veterinary help.

title: Abortion or stillbirth cluster
text: Abortion, stillbirth, or several animals losing pregnancy should be treated as a herd-health and possible public-health warning. Do not handle fetal material or discharge casually. Keep people, children, dogs, and other animals away, preserve the situation for trained assessment if safe, and contact local animal-health support. Do not guess the disease from text.

title: Straining to urinate or no urine
text: Repeated straining to urinate, little or no urine, crying, belly pain, swollen belly, or a male goat/sheep unable to pass urine is urgent. Do not give random diuretics, salt, soda, alcohol, or force water. Keep the animal calm and call trained veterinary help quickly.

title: Seizure, tremor, circling, or head pressing
text: Seizures, tremors, circling, head pressing, paralysis, sudden behavior change, or repeated falling are urgent signs. Causes can include poisoning, infection, injury, metabolic illness, or other serious problems. Do not diagnose from text, restrain roughly, force liquids, or give medicines yourself. Keep people safe and call trained help.

title: Sudden death and carcass handling boundary
text: Sudden unexplained death, especially with bleeding from body openings, no stiffness, or multiple deaths, can be a public-health warning. Do not cut open, butcher, skin, eat, sell, or move the carcass casually. Keep people and animals away and contact official animal-health authorities or a veterinarian.

title: Do not open suspected disease carcass
text: Do not open, cut, skin, or process an animal that died suddenly or may have infectious disease. Some carcass handling can expose people and contaminate the area. Do not give meat or milk safety clearance from text. Keep the area away from children, dogs, and other animals and seek official guidance.

title: Heat stroke emergency signs
text: Heat stress becomes urgent when the animal is panting or breathing very fast, drooling, weak, staggering, collapsed, not drinking, or several animals are affected during hot humid weather. Move only if safe and gentle, provide shade/airflow and clean water access. Do not force water into the mouth of a weak or collapsed animal. Get trained help quickly.

title: Choking or blocked throat suspicion
text: If feed seems stuck in the throat, the animal cannot swallow, coughs repeatedly while eating, has froth/saliva, distress, or belly swelling, treat it as urgent. Do not push sticks, pipes, bottles, oil, or hands deep into the mouth/throat. Keep the animal calm and call trained help.

title: Severe breathing trouble
text: Open-mouth breathing, gasping, blue/pale tongue or gums, severe fast breathing, collapse, or breathing trouble with not eating is urgent. Keep the animal calm in an airy, low-stress place. Do not force water, oil, medicine, or feed into the mouth. Contact trained veterinary help immediately.

title: Cough or breathing signs in several animals
text: Cough, nasal discharge, fever, or breathing trouble in several animals is a herd/flock warning. Do not mix animals casually, take them to market, or give random antibiotics. Improve ventilation if safe, observe how many are affected, and contact local animal-health support.

title: Severe dehydration warning
text: Loose dung with severe weakness, sunken eyes, very dry mouth, not drinking, cold ears/legs, repeated watery diarrhea, or inability to stand is urgent. Do not give human medicine, antibiotic, or unknown electrolyte dose yourself. Keep clean water accessible if the animal can drink normally and arrange trained help.

title: Bloody or severe diarrhea
text: Bloody, black, foul-smelling, repeated watery diarrhea, or diarrhea with fever, weakness, dehydration, or not eating is a red flag. Do not give antibiotics, anti-diarrhea tablets, human ORS dose, or home mixtures as treatment instructions. Keep the animal clean and seek trained help.

title: Goat or sheep mouth sores with diarrhea/cough
text: In goats or sheep, mouth sores with fever, nasal/eye discharge, cough, pneumonia-like signs, diarrhea, or several affected animals should be treated as a flock-health warning. Do not diagnose the exact disease from text, sell or mix affected animals casually, or give random antibiotics. Contact animal-health support.

title: Goat or sheep birth trouble
text: For goat or sheep birth trouble, visible water bag with no progress, abnormal position, severe straining, weakness, or stuck kid/lamb needs trained help. Do not pull hard, insert hands repeatedly, or use oil/soap/internal remedies as instructions. Keep the area clean and calm and arrange help.

title: Poultry sudden deaths or bird flu concern
text: Many poultry dying suddenly, severe respiratory signs, swelling, blue comb/wattles, or bird-flu concern should be treated as a flock/public-health warning. Do not sell, slaughter, eat, or move sick/dead birds casually. Keep children away, avoid bare-hand handling, and contact local animal-health authorities.

title: Chemical burn or caustic exposure
text: Chemical exposure, acid/caustic contact, kerosene/phenyl application, or burn-like skin injury needs careful safety handling. Keep people safe and avoid adding more chemicals or irritants. Do not neutralize with random substances. Move away from the source if safe and seek trained help, especially near eyes, mouth, udder, or deep skin injury.

title: No meat or milk clearance in dangerous disease situations
text: Do not give meat or milk safety clearance when there is sudden death, suspected infectious disease, abnormal milk, poisoning, bite/saliva exposure, or multiple sick animals. The safe response is to keep products separate, avoid sale/use, and seek official veterinary or public-health guidance.
